1:
“The book of the generation of Jesus Christ, the son of David, the son of Abraham.” The witnesses open the New Testament with the human genealogy of Christ, and dwell on its title and its theological weight. The Latin fathers, with Jerome, note that Matthew, signified by “the face of a man” in Ezekiel’s vision, fitly “opens his Gospel with the human genealogy of Christ,” and that Isaiah’s “Who shall declare his generation?” (Isaiah 53:8) speaks of the divine generation, not contradicted here. Ambrose marks the naming of “two authors of His birth” — David and Abraham — the heads of the kingly and the covenant lines. The grammatical-historical commentators attend to the words: Robertson notes “the Book” (biblos, our “Bible”), and Henry that “the book of the generation” (biblos geneseōs) is the Hebrew manner of titling a narrative of descent. Lightfoot supplies the Jewish background of carefully kept genealogies.

The deepest stress falls on the person of the One whose genealogy this is. The Eastern Chrysostom calls the title “the sum of the whole dispensation, the root of all its blessings — that God became man.” Against the heresies that made Christ a mere man — Cerinthus, Ebion, Paul of Samosata (Augustine) — the Eastern Alexandrian voices set his eternal deity: Pseudo-Athanasius cites “In the beginning was the Word” (John 1:1), and Cyril of Alexandria, “who being in the form of God thought it not robbery to be equal with God” (Philippians 2:6). Cyril of Jerusalem holds both together: “the Son of David at the end of the ages, but the Son of God before all ages.” Calvin, for the Reformed, opens the much-debated question whether Matthew and Luke both trace the line through Joseph, or Matthew through Joseph and Luke through Mary. The witnesses thus find in the opening verse the great theme of the Gospel: the eternal Son of God, true God before all ages, has become true man, the son of David and of Abraham, entering the human line to fulfil the promises made to the fathers — his genealogy according to the flesh the seal of a real incarnation.
2:
“Abraham begat Isaac; and Isaac begat Jacob; and Jacob begat Judas and his brethren.” The witnesses note the chosen line and the names passed over. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that only the direct line of promise is traced: Robertson observes that “begat” (egennēsen) runs with regularity to v.16, where the form changes at the birth of Jesus, and that it “does not always mean immediate parentage, but direct descent.” Gill, Clarke, and Ellicott note why Ishmael and Esau are omitted — they were “outside the covenant,” for “in Isaac shall thy seed be called” (Genesis 21:12), and Esau “had forfeited both the birthright and the blessing.” Yet “Judah and his brethren” are all named, the twelve patriarchs being together the people of God.

The fathers read the names mystically. The Latin Ambrose marks Abraham as “the first who deserved the witness of faith,” who “believed God, and it was counted to him for righteousness” (Genesis 15:6). The Eastern Pseudo-Chrysostom finds Christ figured in the names: Isaac, “laughter,” the joy of the heart; Jacob, “supplanter”; Judah, “confessor,” a type of Christ who confessed the Father (Matthew 11:25). Calvin, for the Reformed, notes that the twelve patriarchs are all given a place, “on all of whom God had bestowed a similar favour of adoption,” the blessing extending to the whole chosen people, not Judah alone. The witnesses thus find in the verse both history and grace. The genealogy follows not the firstborn by nature but the line of promise, chosen by God’s free election — Isaac not Ishmael, Jacob not Esau — so that from the first the descent of Christ is marked by sovereign grace. And the naming of “Judah and his brethren” shows that, though the Messiah springs from Judah alone, the whole house of Israel is gathered into the covenant story that leads to him.

The witnesses observe that the genealogy thus begins where the promises began. From Abraham, to whom it was said ‘in thee shall all families of the earth be blessed,’ the line of blessing runs through the chosen sons to Christ, in whom the promise is fulfilled. The verse therefore is no dry list but the tracing of a covenant: each ‘begat’ carries forward the pledge made to the fathers, until the Seed should come in whom all nations are blessed. The reader is taught to see, in the bare succession of names, the patient faithfulness of God keeping his word across the generations.
3:
“And Judas begat Phares and Zara of Thamar; and Phares begat Esrom; and Esrom begat Aram.” The witnesses note especially the naming of Tamar, the first of the women in the genealogy. The Latin fathers dwell on the surprise of it: Jerome observes that “none of the holy women are taken into the Saviour’s genealogy, but rather such as Scripture has condemned,” so that “He who came for sinners being born of sinners might put away the sins of all.” Ambrose contrasts Matthew with Luke, who “avoided the mention of these, that he might set forth the series of the priestly race immaculate,” while Matthew’s plan did not exclude “the righteousness of natural reason.” The grammatical-historical commentators supply the history: the Gloss notes that Judah “begat Phares and Zarah before he went into Egypt,” and the line runs on through Esrom and Aram (cf. Genesis 38:29; Ruth 4:18).

The witnesses dwell on the grace displayed in the women’s names. The genealogy of the holy Saviour includes Tamar, whose union with Judah was shameful, as later it will name Rahab the harlot, Ruth the Moabitess, and the wife of Uriah — none of them chosen for their honour, several of them Gentiles or sinners. The fathers and the commentators alike read in this a deliberate design: that the Messiah, who came to save sinners, should spring from a line that included sinners and strangers, so that his very pedigree preaches the gospel of grace. The witnesses thus find in the mention of Tamar a quiet but striking testimony. The Evangelist does not hide the stains in Christ’s ancestry but records them, that the reader may see from the outset that the Saviour entered a fallen race not as one ashamed of it, but as one who came to redeem it. The women whom Scripture had marked with shame are taken up into the lineage of the sinless One, foreshadowing the mercy by which he would gather sinners and Gentiles into the family of God.

The witnesses add that the inclusion of Tamar guards the genealogy from every boast of human merit. Had the line been adorned only with the names of the great and the holy, men might have thought the Saviour came of a worthy stock; but the naming of the shameful teaches that he came of grace alone, stooping to a race of sinners. So the verse, in a single name, overturns all pride of ancestry and prepares the reader for a Christ who is the friend of sinners, born among them that he might save them.
4:
“And Aram begat Aminadab; and Aminadab begat Naasson; and Naasson begat Salmon.” The witnesses note that this stretch of the genealogy carries the line through the generation of the Exodus and the wilderness. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the history: Naasson (Nahshon), Ellicott and Wesley observe, was “prince of the tribe of Judah” at the time of the Exodus and the entry into Canaan, “the brother of Elisheba the wife of Aaron” (Numbers 1:7; 1 Chronicles 2:10). Gill notes that the succession here “exactly agrees with the genealogical account” of the Old Testament (Ruth 4:19–20), confirming the accuracy of the record.

The witnesses dwell on the quiet faithfulness the verse records. Through the bondage in Egypt, the deliverance under Moses, and the long wandering, the line of promise is preserved unbroken: from Aram to Aminadab to Naasson, the prince who led Judah in the wilderness, to Salmon, who would enter the land. The witnesses find in these otherwise obscure names a testimony to the providence of God, who kept the chosen seed through every change of the nation’s fortunes, that the promise to Abraham and Judah might in due time be fulfilled. The genealogy, the witnesses note, is not a list of the famous but a chain of grace, each link holding fast the covenant pledge across the centuries. That the names agree exactly with the records of the Law and the Prophets shows the Evangelist building on the sure foundation of Scripture; and that the line runs through the prince of Judah at the founding of the nation marks the steady advance of the purpose of God toward the King who should at last be born of Judah’s house.

The witnesses observe that the very obscurity of these names magnifies the patience of God. No great deeds are recorded of Aram or Aminadab; they are remembered only as links in the chain that bears the promise. Yet each was necessary, and each was kept. The verse thus teaches that the purposes of God are carried forward as much by the unknown and the ordinary as by the famous, and that the long, quiet faithfulness of unremarkable lives has its place in the story of redemption. The Messiah came of a line in which most names are little more than names — and God forgot none of them.
5:
“And Salmon begat Booz of Rachab; and Booz begat Obed of Ruth; and Obed begat Jesse.” The witnesses note that two more women — both outsiders — are named here. The grammatical-historical commentators mark the surprise of “Rachab”: Gill observes that, while the Old Testament says Salmon begat Boaz (Ruth 4:21), it is the Evangelist who tells us his mother was “Rahab, that is, Rahab the harlot” of Jericho (Joshua 6:25). Lightfoot notes that the Jewish writers too held Rahab married into Israel. Then “Obed of Ruth” — Ruth the Moabitess (Ruth 4:13). Ellicott observes that the Old Testament is silent on the marriage of Salmon with the harlot, the fact “probably thought of as a blot rather than a glory.”

The witnesses dwell on the grace these names proclaim. After Tamar (v.3) come Rahab, a Gentile harlot, and Ruth, a Moabitess — both strangers to the covenant by birth, both gathered into the line of the Messiah by faith. The witnesses find in this a deliberate foreshadowing of the gospel: the Saviour who would call sinners and Gentiles into the family of God springs from a line that already, in its women, embraced both. Rahab, saved by faith from the doom of Jericho, and Ruth, who left her gods to cleave to the God of Israel, stand in the ancestry of Christ as living proofs that grace reaches beyond Israel to the nations and beyond the righteous to the fallen. Wesley adds a note of providence in the long-lived succession of Salmon, Boaz, and Obed. The witnesses thus read the verse as another quiet sermon woven into the genealogy: the pedigree of the holy Saviour is studded with the names of Gentile and sinful women, that his descent itself might declare him the Redeemer of all who come to God by faith.

The witnesses add that the gathering of Rahab and Ruth into the line prepares the reader for the wideness of Christ’s mercy. The Saviour whose pedigree includes a Canaanite harlot and a Moabite widow could not be a Saviour for the Jews alone. From the first, his descent declares that the door of grace stands open to the Gentile and the outcast who come to God by faith. The verse thus weaves the gospel into the genealogy, so that the names of two foreign women become a promise of the nations’ ingathering through the Son of David they helped to bring forth.
6:
“And Jesse begat David the king; and David the king begat Solomon of her that had been the wife of Urias.” The witnesses note the royal title given to David and the veiled naming of Bathsheba. The Latin fathers dwell on the latter: Remigius asks why Matthew does not name Bathsheba as he named the other women, and answers that the others, “though deserving of much blame, were yet commendable for many virtues,” while she shared in the great sin; the Gloss adds that, by naming “Urias” instead, the Evangelist “recalls to memory that great wickedness.” Ambrose praises David, who “confessed himself to be but man” and washed out his sin “with the tears of repentance.” Augustine reads the descent through Solomon as fitting Matthew’s theme of Christ “taking on him our sins,” David having sinned in Solomon’s mother.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators note the emphatic “the king.” Calvin observes that “the designation of king is bestowed on David alone, because in his person God exhibited a type of the future leader of his people, the Messiah”; Wesley agrees it is given “because his throne is given to the Messiah” (cf. Luke 1:32). Gill notes that David, “the youngest of Jesse’s sons… despised by his brethren,” was chosen and anointed by God. The witnesses thus find in the verse the meeting of royalty and grace. The genealogy reaches its kingly height in David, the type of Christ the King, whose throne the Messiah would inherit for ever; yet at that very height it records the darkest stain — the sin with the wife of Uriah — naming the wronged husband to keep the guilt in view. The witnesses read in this both warning and comfort: the royal line of the Saviour passes through a repentant sinner, so that the King who comes of David’s house is shown to be the King who saves sinners, of whom David, fallen and forgiven, is at once the ancestor and the type.

The witnesses dwell further on the union of majesty and mercy in this verse. The genealogy reaches its royal summit in David the king, yet at that summit it records his deepest fall; the throne destined for the Messiah is reached through a path of sin and repentance. So the verse holds together the two things the gospel joins: that Christ is the King of David’s line, and that he is the Saviour of David’s kind — sinners who, like David, are restored by grace. The crown and the confession stand side by side, and the King who comes of this house comes to forgive.
7:
“And Solomon begat Roboam; and Roboam begat Abia; and Abia begat Asa.” The witnesses carry the line from the height of the kingdom into its division and decline. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the history: Gill notes that Roboam (Rehoboam) was born of “Naamah an Ammonitess” (1 Kings 14:21), and the succession agrees with the chronicles of the kings (1 Chronicles 3:10). The Eastern Pseudo-Chrysostom finds meaning in the names: Solomon, “the peacemaker,” who reigned in peace over the subdued nations, and Roboam, “by a multitude of people,” a figure read into the unfolding line.

The witnesses dwell on the mingled character of the royal succession. From Solomon in his glory the line passes to Rehoboam, in whose reign the kingdom was rent; to Abijah; and to Asa, a king who, for all his faults, did much that was right. The witnesses note that the genealogy of Christ runs not through an unbroken line of saints but through kings good and bad, faithful and faithless alike — the peacemaker Solomon, the foolish Rehoboam who lost the ten tribes, the reforming Asa. They find in this a testimony to the persistence of grace: God carries forward his purpose through a line marked by sin and failure as much as by faithfulness, keeping the promise to David’s house even when David’s sons forsook the LORD. The witnesses thus read these names as a record of patient mercy. The throne of David, pledged to the Messiah, descends through a history of decline and division, yet is never abandoned; and the very unworthiness of many in the line magnifies the faithfulness of the God who, despite all, brought forth from David’s house the King who should reign for ever.

The witnesses press the comfort that the covenant outlasts the failures of those who carry it. Rehoboam’s folly tore the kingdom, yet the promise to David’s house held; Asa’s reforms could not undo every evil, yet the line ran on. The verse thus assures the reader that the coming of the Saviour was never in jeopardy through the sins of his ancestors, for it rested not on them but on the faithfulness of God. The throne of David passed through weak and unworthy hands, yet was kept inviolate for the King whom no failure could prevent.
8:
“And Asa begat Josaphat; and Josaphat begat Joram; and Joram begat Ozias.” The witnesses note that three kings are silently passed over between Joram and Ozias, and debate the reason. The grammatical-historical commentators observe the omission: Lightfoot and Clarke note that “the names of Ahaziah, Joash, and Amaziah are struck out” (cf. 1 Chronicles 3:11–12), so that Joram “begat” Ozias (Uzziah) only mediately — Wesley comparing it to the way “Christ is mediately the son of David and of Abraham.” The Latin fathers offer reasons of judgment: Augustine holds the three were “excluded… because their wickedness was continuous and without interval”; Pseudo-Chrysostom and Hilary connect it to the curse on the house of Ahab, whose blood entered the line through Athaliah, so that “the stain of the Gentile alliance being purged, the royal race is again taken up in the fourth following generation.”

The witnesses dwell on the meaning of the deliberate omission. The three kings cut from the list were descendants, through Athaliah, of the idolatrous house of Ahab and Jezebel, on which God had pronounced that he would cut off every male to the fourth generation; their excision from the genealogy of Christ, the fathers find, is the working-out of that curse, the polluted Gentile alliance purged from the royal line. The witnesses note the sobering lesson: that persistent wickedness may blot a name even from the recorded ancestry of the Messiah, while the line of promise flows on through those whom God preserves. Yet they observe too that the omission serves the Evangelist’s ordered structure of three fourteens (v.17), the genealogy being arranged for memory rather than exhaustive completeness. The witnesses thus find in the struck-out names both a token of divine judgment on apostasy and a mark of the genealogy’s design — a record not of every link but of the chosen succession by which God brought his promise to its appointed end in Christ.

The witnesses draw the double lesson of the struck-out names. On the one hand, the omission warns that persistent, unrepented wickedness may erase a man even from the honoured record, the line of grace flowing past those who set themselves against God. On the other, the ordering of the list into measured generations shows the Evangelist’s care to make the genealogy memorable and clear. Both the judgment and the design serve one end: to display the chosen succession by which, despite the apostasy of many, God brought his promise unbroken to its fulfilment in Christ.
9:
“And Ozias begat Joatham; and Joatham begat Achaz; and Achaz begat Ezekias.” The witnesses carry the line through a mixed succession of kings and note again the shaping of the list. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that “Ozias” is the Uzziah of the Old Testament (struck with leprosy for his presumption, 2 Chronicles 26:16), and recall, with Jamieson-Fausset-Brown and Ellicott, that three kings have just been omitted (Ahaziah, Joash, Amaziah) so as “to compress the whole into three fourteens” (v.17). Gill supplies the parentage of Jotham, born of Jerushah the daughter of Zadok (2 Chronicles 27:1).

The witnesses dwell on the contrasts within this short span of the line. Jotham was a good king; Ahaz, his son, one of the worst, who burned his children to idols and shut up the house of the LORD; yet from this faithless king came Hezekiah, among the best and most godly of all the kings of Judah. The witnesses find in this jarring succession — good father, wicked son, godly grandson — a testimony to the freedom and faithfulness of grace, which is not bound by the merit or demerit of the fathers but keeps its own course toward the promised King. They note that the line of the Messiah passes through an Ahaz as well as a Hezekiah, so that no man may boast of his ancestry nor despair of his descent; the purpose of God runs on through the worst as through the best. The witnesses thus read the verse as another quiet proof that the coming of Christ rests not on the worthiness of his forebears but on the unfailing covenant of God, who through a chequered line of kings, faithful and apostate by turns, preserved the seed of David until the Son of David should be born.

The witnesses find in this jarring run of names a parable of free grace. That a godly Hezekiah should spring from a wicked Ahaz, and that the line should pass through kings so unlike in character, teaches that the descent of the Saviour rested on no merit of the fathers. God kept his covenant through the faithful and the faithless alike, so that the coming of Christ is owed wholly to his own unfailing purpose. The verse thus forbids both the boast of a worthy ancestry and the despair of an unworthy one, fixing the believer’s hope on the God who keeps his word across every generation.
10:
“And Ezekias begat Manasses; and Manasses begat Amon; and Amon begat Josias.” The witnesses note the descent through Manasseh, the most notorious of Judah’s kings, to the godly Josiah. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the history: Gill observes that Hezekiah begat Manasseh of Hephzibah (2 Kings 21:1; cf. 2 Chronicles 33:1), a king “very remarkable both for his sins, and for his humiliation on account of them,” and the line passes through Amon to Josiah the reformer.

The witnesses dwell on the marvel of grace in Manasseh’s place in the line. He filled Jerusalem with idolatry and innocent blood, the worst of the kings; yet, carried captive to Babylon, he humbled himself greatly before God and was restored (2 Chronicles 33:12–13). The witnesses find in his name, set in the ancestry of the holy Saviour, a striking testimony to the depth of divine mercy: the chief of sinners among the kings, brought to repentance, stands in the genealogy of Christ. They note the contrast with the kings struck out for their wickedness (v.8) — that Manasseh, though far worse in deed, is retained, perhaps because he repented where they did not, so that even the genealogy distinguishes the penitent from the obdurate. The witnesses thus read the verse as a further sermon of grace woven into the list of names: that the line of the Redeemer should include a Manasseh, the idolater turned penitent, declares from the outset that no sinner is beyond the reach of the mercy that came in Christ, and that the King born of this line came to seek and to save the very worst.

The witnesses press the wonder that Manasseh, the worst of the kings, holds a place in the holy line, while others were struck out. The difference, they suggest, lay in repentance: the idolater who humbled himself was kept, the obdurate were cut off. So the verse preaches that no depth of sin is beyond recovery where there is true penitence, and that the line of the Redeemer was made to include a Manasseh that none might think himself too great a sinner for the grace which came in Christ. The pedigree of the sinless Saviour is, by design, a record of mercy to the guilty.
11:
“And Josias begat Jechonias and his brethren, about the time they were carried away to Babylon.” The witnesses note the difficulties in this verse and the great turning-point it marks. The grammatical-historical commentators discuss the problems Clarke and Ellicott raise: that “Josias was not the father of Jechonias” but his grandfather, a link (Eliakim/Jehoiakim) being omitted (2 Kings 23:34; 1 Chronicles 3:15–16); and that “his brethren” are, as Wesley notes, more strictly his uncles, “the Jews terming all kinsmen brethren.” An alternative reading inserts the missing Jakim. Gill and Lightfoot work through the sons of Josiah to identify Jechonias.

The witnesses dwell on the solemn note of the deportation — “about the time they were carried away to Babylon.” Here the kingdom of David, traced through all its kings from its glory under David and Solomon, sinks into captivity; the crown falls, the throne is empty, the nation exiled. The witnesses find in this the lowest point of the genealogy, the royal line brought down to a captive remnant. Remigius (in v.8) noted that some were “born for this purpose, that they should be led captive”; the dominion has passed away. Yet the witnesses observe that the line is not broken: through the captivity itself the seed of David is preserved, so that the promise should not fail. They thus read the verse as the hinge of the genealogy’s three ages — from Abraham to David the rise, from David to the captivity the decline, and from the captivity to Christ the restoration (v.17). The carrying away to Babylon is the dark valley through which the line of promise passes; but even there God keeps the royal seed, that out of the fallen house of David the King should at last be born.

The witnesses observe that the genealogy does not flinch from the nation’s deepest humiliation. It names the carrying away to Babylon openly, marking the place where the kingdom fell and the crown was lost. Yet by recording the captivity within the line, the Evangelist shows that even the exile served the purpose of God, who preserved the royal seed in the very ruin of the kingdom. The verse thus teaches that the lowest points of the people’s history were not the failure of the promise but the dark road by which it advanced, until from the fallen house of David the everlasting King should come.
12:
“And after they were brought to Babylon, Jechonias begat Salathiel; and Salathiel begat Zorobabel.” The witnesses note the resuming of the line after the exile and a knot of difficulties. The Latin fathers raise the problem of Jeremiah’s word against Coniah: Ambrose asks how it was said “there shall not spring of his seed one sitting on the throne of David” (Jeremiah 22:30), if Christ both reigned and was of Jechonias’ seed. The grammatical-historical commentators answer with Jamieson-Fausset-Brown that the prophecy meant only that no man of his seed should prosper “sitting upon the throne of David” in the old earthly sense — the kingdom being then ended — not that he should be childless absolutely (so Salathiel, 1 Chronicles 3:17). Lightfoot reads Salathiel as “a son of the kingdom,” the Davidic dignity “altogether withered in the rest of the sons of Josiah” but somewhat reviving in him; Ellicott notes the cluster of genealogical difficulties here and the apparent variance with the Chronicles.

Calvin, for the Reformed, marks the great reversal the verse records: after the captivity “the descendants of David, from being kings, then became exiles and slaves,” and that captivity, “a sort of destruction,” was yet “wonderfully arranged by Divine providence” to preserve the seed. The Latin Remigius and Rabanus read the post-exilic names mystically as figures of Christ — Zorobabel and the rest pointing to him. The witnesses thus find in the verse the turning of the genealogy from descent to recovery. The royal line, sunk into bondage in Babylon, is not extinguished there: out of the captive house of Jechonias spring Salathiel and Zorobabel, through whom the seed of David is carried home. The witnesses note that the very prophecy which seemed to bar a king of Coniah’s line was fulfilled in a deeper sense — no earthly successor prospered on David’s throne, yet from that line came the King whose throne is not of this world. The exile that looked like the end of the promise was the dark passage through which God preserved it.

The witnesses add that the verse quietly answers the seeming defeat of the exile. To the eye of the age, the carrying away to Babylon had cancelled the promise to David’s house; no son of his sat on the throne, and the prophets had pronounced the line cut off. Yet through Salathiel and Zorobabel the seed lived on, and Zorobabel led the return and rebuilt the temple. So the verse teaches that the word of God, even when it seems to bury the promise, is in truth preserving it, and that the King would come not in spite of the exile but through it.
13:
“And Zorobabel begat Abiud; and Abiud begat Eliakim; and Eliakim begat Azor.” The witnesses note that here the genealogy passes into the obscure post-exilic generations unrecorded elsewhere in Scripture. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that these names are not found in the Old Testament: Gill notes that the sons of Zorobabel named in the Chronicles are “Meshullam, and Hananiah, and Shelomith their sister,” with “no mention of Abiud,” who “seems to be the same with Meshullam the eldest son, who might have two names” (1 Chronicles 3:19). From here to Joseph the line rests on records other than the canonical histories, drawn from the genealogical registers the Jews carefully kept.

The Latin fathers, finding little history, read the names for their meaning: Remigius takes Abiud as “He is my Father,” after “I and the Father are one” (John 10:30), and Rabanus traces a moral sense through the names, each pointing toward Christ. The witnesses dwell on the silence of these generations. For nearly four hundred years between the return from Babylon and the birth of Christ, the line of David passes through men of whom Scripture tells us nothing — no kings, no prophets, no recorded deeds, only names. Yet the witnesses find in this silent stretch a testimony to the hidden faithfulness of God. Through the long years when the voice of prophecy had ceased and the house of David had sunk into obscurity, the seed of promise was still being carried, generation by generation, toward its appointed end. The witnesses thus read these unknown names as a record of God’s quiet keeping: that even in the silent centuries, when nothing seemed to be happening and the royal line lay hidden among ordinary men, the purpose of redemption advanced unbroken toward the King who should be born of this very house.

The witnesses note that the church may take comfort from these forgotten names. If God remembered and used generations whose deeds are nowhere recorded, then no faithful but obscure life is lost to his purpose. The silent centuries between the Testaments, when heaven seemed quiet and the house of David unknown, were in truth the time of God’s patient preparation. The verse thus assures the believer that the hidden years are not wasted years, and that the Lord advances his kingdom as surely through the unseen and the unremembered as through the famous and the great.
14:
“And Azor begat Sadoc; and Sadoc begat Achim; and Achim begat Eliud.” The witnesses note that the genealogy continues through names otherwise unknown to history. The grammatical-historical commentators have little to add beyond the bare succession, these generations belonging to the obscure period between the Testaments, recorded in the Evangelist’s source but nowhere else in Scripture. The names — Azor, Sadoc, Achim, Eliud — stand as silent links in the chain of descent from Zorobabel to Joseph.

The Latin fathers, here as before, find in the very names a hidden testimony, reading their meanings as foreshadowings of Christ and his work, after the manner of Rabanus and Remigius on the surrounding verses. The witnesses dwell on the lesson of these forgotten generations. The men named here did nothing that Scripture thought worth recording; they were neither kings nor prophets nor doers of great deeds, but ordinary descendants of a fallen royal house, living through the long twilight before the dawn of the Messiah. Yet each held his place in the line of promise, and each was necessary to it. The witnesses find here a quiet rebuke to all who measure significance by fame: in the genealogy of the Saviour, the obscure and the unremembered have their honoured place, for through them, no less than through David and Abraham, God carried forward his saving purpose. The silence of these names, the witnesses note, is itself eloquent of the patience of God, who across nameless generations and unrecorded years kept faith with his promise, preparing in obscurity the line from which, in the fullness of time, his Son should be born.

The witnesses press the dignity these obscure links retain. Though Scripture passes them in silence, the Holy Spirit caused their names to be written in the genealogy of the Saviour, so that they share for ever in the honour of his line. The verse thus overturns the world’s reckoning of worth, in which only the notable are remembered, and teaches that in the kingdom of God the lowly and the hidden have an enduring place. Through such as these, no less than through kings, the promise was carried; and their forgotten faithfulness is owned in the record of Christ’s descent.
15:
“And Eliud begat Eleazar; and Eleazar begat Matthan; and Matthan begat Jacob.” The witnesses bring the genealogy to the last generations before Joseph. The grammatical-historical commentators note that these too are names not preserved in the Old Testament, the line running through Eleazar, Matthan, and Jacob (the father of Joseph) toward its goal; Jamieson-Fausset-Brown, looking back over the whole, reaffirms that the succession “after the migration of Babylon” agrees with the record (1 Chronicles 3:17) and that Jeremiah’s word on Coniah (Jeremiah 22:30) is no contradiction.

The witnesses dwell on the approach of the genealogy to its end. After the long line of obscure generations, the names of Matthan and Jacob bring the reader to the threshold of the nativity, for Jacob is the father of Joseph, the husband of Mary. The witnesses note that Matthan, in the harmonising accounts of the fathers (Eusebius, on v.16), figures in the explanation of how Joseph could be called the son of Jacob in Matthew and the son of Heli in Luke. They find in this last stretch of the list the quiet culmination of centuries of patient providence: the seed of David, carried through kings and exiles and nameless generations, has arrived at the household into which the Saviour will be born. The witnesses thus read these closing names as the hush before the great announcement. The genealogy has run its long course from Abraham, through David, through the captivity, through the silent years; and now, in Matthan and Jacob, it stands at the door of Bethlehem, the next verse breaking the long succession of “begats” with the wonder of a birth that came not by man, but of the Virgin, by the Holy Ghost.

The witnesses observe how the long genealogy now gathers to its point. Every name from Abraham onward has been a step toward this household, and in Matthan and Jacob the centuries of waiting reach the eve of fulfilment. So the verse stands as the final approach to the nativity: the seed of the woman, promised in Eden, the seed of Abraham, the son of David, has been carried through every age to the very threshold of his coming. The next words will break the chain of begettings with the wonder for which the whole line was kept — the birth of Jesus, who is called Christ.
16:
“And Jacob begat Joseph the husband of Mary, of whom was born Jesus, who is called Christ.” The witnesses dwell on the sudden, deliberate change of formula that signals the virgin birth. The grammatical-historical commentators note that the long chain of “begat” breaks: not “Joseph begat Jesus,” but “Joseph the husband of Mary, of whom (feminine, of Mary) was born Jesus” — the Evangelist carefully avoiding any word that would make Joseph the natural father (cf. v.18). The Eastern Chrysostom and Gennadius press the point: Joseph is named “the husband of Mary,” included “for her sake,” for “the Son of God was born of human flesh, that is of Mary, and not by man after the way of nature, as Ebion says.” On the discrepancy with Luke — Joseph “son of Jacob” here, “son of Heli” there — Jerome answers that “one was his father by nature, the other by law,” and Eusebius (following Africanus) explains it by a levirate marriage, while Lightfoot and Wesley hold that Luke traces Mary’s line, Matthew Joseph’s.

The Latin fathers and the councils guard the person of Christ. Augustine notes that Joseph is rightly called Jesus’ father by adoption and legal right, though not by flesh. Hilary explains “who is called Christ” of the anointing by the Holy Spirit, by which “when born he was called Christ”; Clarke and Calvin add that “Christ” (the Anointed, answering the Hebrew Messiah) marks his office as the divinely appointed Redeemer. The Council of Ephesus warns against Nestorius, and Pseudo-Augustine against dividing the one Christ, “the same Christ the Son of both God and man.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the hinge of the genealogy and of the Gospel. The whole line has led to Joseph; but Joseph is only “the husband of Mary,” and the Saviour is born not of him but “of her,” by the Holy Ghost. The careful wording both grafts Jesus into the royal line of David through his legal father and guards the mystery of his supernatural conception. The genealogy of the kings ends not in another “begetting” but in a birth like no other — Jesus, who is called Christ, the Son of David by law and the Son of God by nature.
17:
“So all the generations from Abraham to David are fourteen generations; and from David until the carrying away into Babylon are fourteen generations; and from the carrying away into Babylon unto Christ are fourteen generations.” The witnesses note the Evangelist’s ordered division and discuss its purpose and difficulties. The grammatical-historical commentators take the arrangement as a memoria technica: Barnes and Ellicott observe that the three groups of fourteen were “for the purpose of aiding the memory,” a method “common among the Jews,” though “the periods embraced by the three groups were of very unequal length,” and names have been omitted to make the count even. Lightfoot notes the same threefold-fourteen pattern in the Jewish schools. Wesley points out the overlap by which the count is completed — David ends the first fourteen and begins the second, and Jesus ends the third.

The fathers find in the threefold division a deeper meaning. The Eastern Chrysostom and Pseudo-Chrysostom read it of the three changes in the people’s government — under patriarchs and judges, under kings, and after the captivity — “to show that not even by the change of their government were they made better,” but held “the same evil course” throughout. The Latin Remigius finds in fourteen (ten plus four) “the agreement of the Law”—the Decalogue—“and the Gospel”—the four books; Augustine accounts for the count by Jechonias being reckoned twice, at the turning-point of the captivity; the Gloss sees the “sevenfold grace of the Holy Spirit” doubled for soul and body. The witnesses thus find in the verse both a practical and a theological design. Practically, the genealogy is shaped into three memorable epochs — the rise to David, the decline to the exile, the recovery to Christ — each of fourteen generations. Theologically, the division marks the unbroken faithfulness of God across every change of the nation’s fortunes: through patriarchs, kings, and exile alike, the line of promise ran its appointed course, until at the close of the third age the Christ was born, in whom the whole history of Israel reached its goal.

The witnesses draw the devotional lesson from the survey. To trace such a series of generations, through rise and fall and recovery, is to see the long patience of God working steadily toward one end. Each age had its changes, its faithful and its faithless, its glory and its shame; yet through all the promise held its course. The verse thus invites the reader to look back over the whole and adore the providence that, across two thousand years and forty-two generations, kept the line of David unbroken until the appointed time, when God sent forth his Son, born of a woman, to redeem his people.
18:
“Now the birth of Jesus Christ was on this wise: When as his mother Mary was espoused to Joseph, before they came together, she was found with child of the Holy Ghost.” The witnesses note that the Evangelist deliberately breaks the chain of natural begettings to relate a birth like no other. The Eastern Chrysostom and Pseudo-Chrysostom mark that, having said “Jacob begat Joseph,” Matthew now “cuts off the thread,” announcing “some new thing,” lest any “suppose that Christ was born by the law” of nature. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the Jewish setting: Barnes, Clarke, and Lightfoot explain that “espoused” (betrothed) was a binding contract, after which the pair were already “husband” and “wife” though not yet come together, with an interval before the marriage. The decisive words are “of the Holy Ghost”: the child was conceived not by man but by the creative power of the Spirit (Ambrose — “Mary was with child of the Holy Spirit” by his power, not his substance).

The fathers guard both the virginity of the mother and the dignity of her Son. The Eastern Alexandrian voices defend the title “mother of God”: Origen calls Mary “the mother of God, of the Only-begotten,” and Cyril argues against Nestorius that she is not merely “the mother of Christ” but bears the One who is God. The Latin Jerome asks why Christ was conceived of a virgin espoused, and answers: that her family might be known through Joseph, that she might be shielded from the charge of adultery, and that she might have a protector. Augustine notes the wedlock was without carnal desire, befitting the sinless One. Henry, for the Puritan expositors, bids us adore the mystery, “not pry into it.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the foundation of the gospel — the virgin conception of the Son of God. The long genealogy has brought us to a birth that owes nothing to the will of man: Mary, a betrothed virgin, is found with child by the Holy Ghost, so that the Saviour, true man of her substance, is yet conceived without sin, the eternal Son entering our nature by a miracle of grace.
19:
“Then Joseph her husband, being a just man, and not willing to make her a publick example, was minded to put her away privily.” The witnesses dwell on Joseph’s character and the question his conduct raises. The grammatical-historical commentators render “just” (dikaios) as “upright,” a man of “Jewish conscientiousness” (Robertson; cf. Luke 1:6): he would neither marry one he supposed unfaithful nor expose her to “public infamy” (Clarke’s rendering of paradeigmatisai), but sought to “put her away privily,” quietly dissolving the betrothal. Lightfoot and Barnes find in this the meeting of justice and mercy — justice that could not overlook apparent sin, mercy that would not disgrace her.

The fathers debate how Joseph could be “just” if he meant to conceal what he took for sin. The Latin Jerome and Augustine answer that to spare her was itself a higher righteousness — “with great kindness” he would not betray what he alone knew (Augustine), and Ambrose praises him for keeping himself “guiltless of her blood, yet pure from her defilements.” But the Eastern Alexandrian Origen offers a striking alternative: Joseph “sought to put her away because he saw in her a great sacrament, to approach which he thought himself unworthy” — not suspecting sin at all, but withdrawing in reverent awe before the mystery. The witnesses thus find here both a portrait of godly character and a genuine debate. Most read Joseph as a righteous man caught in a painful dilemma, who, believing the worst, chose the gentlest course the law allowed — to release Mary without shame. Origen and a few read him as one who already sensed the holy thing in her and dared not claim her. Either way, the witnesses note the tenderness and integrity of the man God chose to guard his Son: just, yet merciful; troubled, yet unwilling to wound; and soon to be set at rest by the angel’s word.

The witnesses observe that, on every reading, Joseph stands as a model of quiet integrity. He neither rushed to condemn nor hardened his heart, but sought the way that was at once righteous and kind. The verse thus commends the spirit that, faced with another’s seeming fault, refuses both the cruelty of public exposure and the laxity of indifference, and waits, as Joseph waited, upon the will of God. His perplexity, honestly borne, was the soil in which the angel’s revelation could be received.
20:
“But while he thought on these things, behold, the angel of the Lord appeared unto him in a dream, saying, Joseph, thou son of David, fear not to take unto thee Mary thy wife: for that which is conceived in her is of the Holy Ghost.” The witnesses note the timely intervention and its tender address. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that Joseph “did not act hastily” (Barnes) but in his perplexity was met by the angel, who came “as the solution of his doubts” (Ellicott) in a dream — the recognised mode of divine communication. The angel calls him “son of David,” which the Eastern Pseudo-Chrysostom and the Gloss read as recalling “the promise of God to David, that of his seed should Christ be born,” and the “fear not” as banishing his distress.

The fathers and Reformers draw out the doctrine and the comfort. Calvin, for the Reformed, marks how “seasonably the Lord aids his people,” showing that “when he appears not to observe our cares, we are still under his eye.” The Latin Augustine guards the truth against error: though the child is “of the Holy Ghost,” we may not say the Spirit is the “father” of the man Christ as God the Father begot the Word — “such an absurdity the ears of the faithful cannot bear” — for the Spirit is the agent, not the father, of the conception. Ambrose notes that calling Mary Joseph’s “wife” does not rob her of her virginity, the betrothed being so called by Scripture custom. The witnesses thus find in the verse the gracious resolving of Joseph’s anguish. At the very point of his deepest perplexity, God sends his angel to reveal the truth: Mary is no adulteress, but the chosen vessel of a miracle; the child is conceived by the Holy Ghost; and Joseph, far from putting her away, is to take her as his wife and guard the holy thing within her. The “son of David” is summoned to give the Son of God a place in the royal line, and his fear is turned to faith by the word from heaven.
21:
“And she shall bring forth a son, and thou shalt call his name JESUS: for he shall save his people from their sins.” The witnesses find in the name and its reason the very heart of the gospel. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the name: “Jesus” is the Greek form of “Joshua” (Yehoshua), “he shall save,” so that, as Jerome says, the etymology is given in the next clause — “for he shall save his people from their sins” (Barnes, Clarke). Pseudo-Chrysostom notes the angel says not “shall bear thee a son,” as to Zacharias, but simply “shall bring forth a son,” since this child is not Joseph’s; and Rabanus marks “thou shalt call his name” — not “give him a name” — for “his name had been given from all eternity.”

The witnesses dwell on the meaning of his saving work. The Latin Remigius stresses that “he saves… not the unbelieving, but his people; those that believe on him,” and “not so much from visible” enemies as from their sins. The Eastern Chrysologus presses the deity implied: “He shall save his people from their sins” — and “it is God that forgives sins,” so that the name proclaims the child to be God. The witnesses thus find in the verse the definition of Christ’s mission, set against every earthly expectation. The Jews looked for a Messiah to save them from the Romans; the angel announces a Saviour who shall save “from their sins” — the deeper bondage, the real ruin. The name Jesus, given from eternity and now bestowed at his birth, declares both who he is and what he came to do: he is the LORD who saves, and his salvation is from sin. The witnesses note that the whole gospel is folded into this one verse — the Saviour’s name, his people, and his work — and that the deliverance he brings reaches not the body and the nation only, but the soul, redeeming his people from the guilt and power of their sins.

The witnesses press the personal reach of the promise. The Saviour comes to save ‘his people from their sins’ — a salvation that meets the deepest need of every soul, not the outward distresses men most fear. The verse thus searches the reader’s hope: many would have a Christ to deliver them from poverty, sickness, or oppression, but the angel announces a Christ who delivers from sin itself. To know him truly is to want, above all, this salvation; and the name JESUS, rightly understood, both reveals the disease and names the only Physician.
22:
“Now all this was done, that it might be fulfilled which was spoken of the Lord by the prophet, saying…” The witnesses note the Evangelist’s own comment, marking the birth as the fulfilment of prophecy. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that this is “the custom of the Evangelist to confirm what he says out of the Old Testament” (Remigius), for the sake of Jewish believers, “that they might recognize as fulfilled in the grace of the Gospel the things foretold.” Robertson discusses the phrase “that it might be fulfilled” (hina plērōthē): the older grammarians (Alford) took it strictly as “in order that,” though modern grammarians recognise a looser use; the point, Ellicott adds, is the correspondence the Evangelist “could not possibly regard… as accidental.” Barnes supplies the context of the prophecy (Isaiah 7:14), delivered to Ahaz some 740 years before.

The fathers find in the formula the testimony of the whole Old Testament to Christ. The Eastern Chrysostom hears the angel comprising in this saying “the depths of the Divine mercy, the laws of nature broken through, and reconciliation made — He who was above all made lower than all.” Calvin, for the Reformed, warns against trifling that confines the prophecy to the bare name; it includes “whatever is heavenly and divine” in Christ. The witnesses thus find in the verse the Evangelist’s key for reading the nativity: it is no isolated marvel but the fulfilment of the prophetic word, the long-promised sign now given. Matthew pauses his narrative to point the believing reader back to Isaiah, that he may see in the virgin’s child the very thing the LORD had foretold to the house of David in the dark days of Ahaz. The witnesses note how this sets the pattern for the whole Gospel, which again and again shows the events of Christ’s life answering to the Scriptures — so that the faith of the Jew, and of every reader, might rest on the harmony of promise and fulfilment, the God of the prophets keeping his word in the birth of his Son.

The witnesses note how this verse shapes the reader’s whole approach to the Gospel. Matthew will not let the nativity be seen as a bare wonder, but ties it at once to the prophetic word, teaching that the God who acts in Christ is the God who spoke through the prophets. The verse thus binds the two Testaments together, and assures the believer that the coming of the Saviour was no afterthought but the keeping of an ancient promise, written centuries before in the dark days of Ahaz and now fulfilled to the letter.
23:
“Behold, a virgin shall be with child, and shall bring forth a son, and they shall call his name Emmanuel, which being interpreted is, God with us.” The witnesses defend the prophecy’s reference to Christ and unfold the name Emmanuel. The grammatical-historical commentators argue, with Gill and Lightfoot, that the words of Isaiah 7:14 can be understood “of no other” than the virgin Mary and her son — not of Ahaz’s wife or Hezekiah, already born. On the disputed Hebrew word, the Latin Jerome insists that since “the Lord himself shall give you a sign,” it “ought to be something new and wonderful,” not the ordinary birth of “a young woman”; only a true virgin’s conception is a sign worthy of the name. Ellicott grants the difficulty of tracing Matthew’s sense in the literal Hebrew, while Wesley explains the Hebrew idiom by which “they shall call his name” means “he shall really and effectually be what he is called.”

The deepest weight falls on “Emmanuel, God with us.” Theodotus, against Photinus, presses that the child “now born” is not “mere man” but God; Calvin, for the Reformed, explains that the name teaches “the manner in which God is with us” — not merely by power and aid, as elsewhere, but in the person of the incarnate Son, dwelling in our flesh. The witnesses thus find in the verse the prophetic seal of the incarnation. The sign given to the house of David was a virgin’s child whose very name declares his nature: Emmanuel, God with us. The witnesses note the union of the two truths the name holds — that he is God, and that he is with us, in our nature, on our side. The Saviour named Jesus (v.21), who saves his people from their sins, is the same Emmanuel of Isaiah, God come to dwell among men; and the believer reads in the ancient prophecy, fulfilled in the manger, the wonder of a God who is not far off but with us, having taken our flesh that he might save us in it.

The witnesses add that the name Emmanuel crowns the genealogy and the nativity alike. The long line of David has led not merely to a king but to God-with-us; the virgin’s child is the LORD come down to dwell among his people. So the verse lifts the reader from the wonder of a miraculous birth to the deeper wonder of the incarnation, in which the eternal God takes our nature to be with us and for us. Emmanuel is the gospel in a single word: God himself, no longer distant, but present in the flesh to save.
24:
“Then Joseph being raised from sleep did as the angel of the Lord had bidden him, and took unto him his wife.” The witnesses note Joseph’s prompt and exact obedience. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that he “promptly obeyed the angel” (Robertson), and that “took unto him his wife” (parelaben tēn gynaika) means he took her home, completing the marriage — Ellicott noting the words “cover a great deal,” the formal ratification of the betrothal, the benediction, the marriage-feast, and the bringing of Mary into his house. Calvin, for the Reformed, finds in “the ready performance” a proof “of the certainty of Joseph’s faith” as well as “his obedience,” for had any scruple remained he “would not have moved a step.”

The fathers draw the lesson of obedience. The Gloss notes that Joseph “not only did what the angel commanded, but as he commanded it,” and bids every one warned of God “break off all delays, rise from sleep, and do that which is commanded.” The Latin Remigius sets Joseph’s obedience against Adam’s disobedience: “by Adam’s disobedience we were ruined; by Joseph’s obedience we all begin to be recalled to our former condition.” The witnesses thus find in the verse a model of faith expressed in action. Joseph, his doubts resolved by the angel’s word, does not delay or question but immediately does as he was bidden, taking Mary as his wife and so accepting the charge of guarding the Saviour and his mother. The witnesses note the quiet greatness of this obedience — that on it, humanly speaking, hung the sheltering of the Christ-child and the honour of his mother — and they commend in Joseph the pattern of every believer: to hear the word of God and at once obey it, without delay or reserve, trusting the One who speaks. His ready faith stands in honoured contrast to the world’s slowness, and shares, the fathers say, in the great reversal by which obedience undoes the ruin that disobedience wrought.

The witnesses press the contrast between Joseph’s ready obedience and the world’s delay. He rose at once and did all that was commanded, asking nothing for himself, content to serve the purpose of God in quiet faithfulness. The verse thus holds up a pattern for every believer: to receive the word of God not with argument or postponement but with immediate, glad obedience. In Joseph’s prompt rising the church has long seen the beauty of a faith that simply does what its Lord commands, and so becomes the guardian of the holy charge entrusted to it.
25:
“And knew her not till she had brought forth her firstborn son: and he called his name JESUS.” The witnesses note the chaste reserve of Joseph and the long-debated question of Mary’s later children. The grammatical-historical commentators explain “knew her not” as a modest euphemism (Gill) for marital union, the imperfect tense marking that “Joseph lived in continence with Mary till the birth of Jesus” (Robertson). Two points have stirred controversy: the word “firstborn” (which Ellicott and Robertson note is absent from the best manuscripts) and the word “till.” On these turns the question whether Mary had other children afterward.

Here the witnesses divide along confessional lines. The Latin Jerome, against Helvidius, defends Mary’s perpetual virginity, holding that “till” and “firstborn” do not imply she bore other children. The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators are more cautious: Barnes holds “the doctrine of the virginity of Mary before the birth of Jesus” to be scriptural and important, “but the Scriptures do not affirm that she had no children afterwards”; Clarke thinks one “may reasonably and piously believe” she had others. Wesley and Calvin agree, however, that the language does not settle it — Wesley citing that “Michal had no child till the day of her death” (2 Samuel 6:23), where “till” plainly implies nothing after; so “knew her not till” need not mean he knew her afterward. The witnesses thus find in the verse a clear affirmation and a contested inference. The clear affirmation is the virgin birth: Joseph did not know Mary until Jesus was born, so that the conception was wholly of the Holy Ghost, no human father sharing in it. The contested inference — whether Mary remained ever-virgin or bore Joseph children later — the witnesses leave where Scripture leaves it, agreeing that the text neither requires nor forbids the perpetual virginity, and that the great matter is the supernatural birth of the Saviour, to whom Joseph, in obedience, gave the appointed name, JESUS.

The witnesses agree that, whatever be concluded about Mary’s later life, the verse fixes beyond doubt the one thing needful — that the conception of Jesus owed nothing to man. Joseph’s continence guards the miracle: the child is wholly of the Holy Ghost. And the chapter closes as it has run, in obedience, as Joseph gives the babe the name the angel appointed. The genealogy of kings ends not in another begetting but in a Saviour named Jesus, born of a virgin, God with us, come to save his people from their sins.
